items.q: Why does deforestation increase flood risk?
items.correct: Trees intercept rainfall, their roots increase infiltration and transpiration removes water — removing them increases surface runoff and reduces lag time, peaking river levels faster.
items.misconception: Deforestation causes floods because trees normally hold water in their branches forever.
items.random_wrong: Deforestation has no effect on rivers — only rainfall matters.
items.why: Interception is temporary but it delays and reduces runoff. Without forests, water reaches rivers faster and in greater volume — interacting with antecedent soil moisture to drive flood peaks.

items.q: What is the difference between mitigation and adaptation to climate change?
items.correct: Mitigation reduces the causes (cutting greenhouse gas emissions); adaptation reduces the impacts (e.g. building sea walls, drought-resistant crops, early warning systems).
items.misconception: Mitigation and adaptation mean the same thing — both are about stopping climate change.
items.random_wrong: Mitigation is done by HICs and adaptation is done by LICs.
items.why: The distinction is causes vs consequences. Both are needed: mitigation tackles the source; adaptation copes with already-locked-in change. Many LICs adapt because they can't mitigate global emissions alone.

items.q: Why does longshore drift move sediment in only one direction along a coast?
items.correct: The prevailing wind drives swash diagonally up the beach at an angle, but gravity-driven backwash returns sediment straight downhill — creating net movement in the swash direction.
items.misconception: Longshore drift moves sediment back and forth with the tide.
items.random_wrong: Longshore drift only happens during storms.
items.why: It's wave-driven, not tide-driven. The net direction is set by the prevailing wind angle relative to the coast — that's why groynes are placed perpendicular to interrupt it.

items.q: Which line divides upland and lowland Britain?
items.why: Correct answer: Tees-Exe Line. “Watford Line” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Where are softer sedimentary rocks found in UK?
items.why: Correct answer: Southeast England. “Scottish Highlands” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Approximately how many km of coastline does the UK have?
items.why: The real figure is 17820 km. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
